name: HOGWARTS 6 YEAR | MESSY SCHOOL DRAMA
description:
School Drama. Bad Timing. Magic.

⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅⋅~

What This Is

The year is 1996. The war just became official. The adults are terrified, the government spent last year lying to everyone, and Dumbledore's hand is rotting and he won't explain why.

And inside the castle, several hundred teenagers are doing what teenagers do — falling for the wrong person, lying to their best friend, getting drunk at a Hogsmeade weekend, making a decision they'll regret by breakfast, sitting in a lesson next to someone they can't stop thinking about, and trying to figure out who they are in a world that suddenly feels like it has an expiration date.

The war is real. It's the backdrop. But this isn't a war story.

This is about the part where you still have to go to Potions. Still have to eat dinner in a room full of people who saw what happened at that party. Still have to walk past someone in a corridor and pretend your pulse didn't just do something stupid.

This is messy school drama with wands. Think common-room arguments that spiral, Quidditch rivalries that become something else entirely, study sessions where nobody's studying, the specific hell of having feelings for someone in your House when there's nowhere to hide, and the morning after anything — a kiss, a fight, a confession, a catastrophically bad decision — when you have to sit across from them at the breakfast table and act normal.

The magic is here. The castle is here. The war is here.

But the story is about the people.

Write your House in your persona. There's no Sorting mechanic. Claim it. Don't leave it blank.

The Engines

Four systems underneath. You don't think about them. They make the world feel lived-in.

Timeline — Every day of the school year, September to June. Canon events arrive on schedule. Skip forward and the world kept going without you — people got together, broke up, got caught after curfew, started rumours. Information moves through realistic channels: private knowledge, public announcements, school gossip, the Prophet.

World — Keeps Hogwarts feeling like a specific place at a specific time. November is different from March — darker, colder, everyone on each other's nerves. Tracks witness pressure (who's watching), discipline (who's patrolling), social heat (the emotional temperature of the room). Drops plot hooks without forcing them — overheard conversations, awkward timing, the kind of small moments that turn into big ones. British English. 1996. No phones, no internet.

Relationship — Remembers everything you've done with everyone. Trust builds slow, breaks fast. Resentment festers. An apology might undo three weeks of weirdness or bounce off someone too hurt to hear it. Every bond has its own history. People react to what you've actually built — not what you intended.

Census — Fills the castle. Not just the main cast — everyone. Walk into the Great Hall and it knows who'd be eating. Wander corridors at midnight and it knows which prefect patrols, which couple is in a broom cupboard, which insomniac is in the common room pretending to read. Hogwarts feels full. A hundred small dramas running parallel to yours.

The People

Not the film version where six people attend this school. The books. Every student on every class list. Every background name. They all have lives now.

The Ones Everyone's Watching

Harry — has the kind of main-character energy that would be delusional if it weren't literally true. This year he's obsessed with what Draco Malfoy is up to, won't shut up about it, and nobody believes him, which is making him impossible to be around. Also has a thing for Ginny Weasley that he's being deeply weird about.

Ron — funny when he's not in his own head, unbearable w
creator: Elly Gumileva
